author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XII. 1.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “At that time Jesus went on the Sabbath day through the corn; and His disciples were a hungered, and began to pluck theears of corn, and to eat.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke saith, “On a double Sabbath.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what is a double Sabbath?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When the cessation from toil is twofold, both that of the regular Sabbath, and that of another feast coming upon it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they call every cessation from toil, a sabbath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why could He have led them away from it, who foreknew all, unless it had been His will that the Sabbath should be broken?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was His will indeed, but not simply so; wherefore He never breaks it without a cause, but giving reasonable excuses: that He might at once bring the law to an end, and not startle them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But there are occasions on which He even repeals it directly, and not with circumstance: as when He anoints with the clay the eyes of the blind man;as when He saith, “My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He doth so, by this to glorify His own Father, by the other to soothe the infirmity of the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At which last He is laboring here, putting forward as a plea the necessity of nature; although in the case of acknowledged sins, that could not of course ever be an excuse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither may the murderer make his anger a plea, nor the adulterer allege his lust, no, nor any other excuse; but here, by mentioning their hunger, He freed them from all blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou, I pray thee, admire the disciples, how entirely they control themselves, and make no account of the things of the body, but esteem the table of the flesh a secondary thing, and though they have to struggle with continual hunger, do not even so withdraw themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For except hunger had sorely constrained them, they would not have done so much as this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then do the Pharisees?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “When they saw it,” it is said, “they said unto Him, Behold, Thy disciples do that which is not lawful to do upon the Sabbath day.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now here indeed with no great vehemence (yet surely that would have been consistent in them),—nevertheless they are not vehemently provoked, but simply find fault.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when He stretched out the withered hand and healed it,then they were so infuriated, as even to consult together about slaying and destroying Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where nothing great and noble is done, they are calm; but where they see any made whole, they are savage, and fret themselves, and none so intolerable as they are: such enemies are they of the salvation of men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then doth Jesus defend His disciples?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Have ye not read,” saith He, “what David did in the temple,when he was an hungered, himself and all they that were with him? how he entered into the house of God, and did eat the show-bread, which was not lawful for him to eat, neither for them which were with him, but only for the priests?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, whereas in pleading for His disciples, He brings forward David; for Himself, it is the Father.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And observe His reproving manner: “Have ye not read what David did?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For great indeed was that prophet’s glory, so that Peter also afterwards pleading with the Jews, spake on this wise, “Let me freely speak unto you of the patriarch David, that he is both dead and buried.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore doth He not call him by the name of his rank, either on this occasion or afterwards?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Perhaps because He derived His race from him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now had they been a candid sort of persons, He would have turned His discourse to the disciples’ suffering from hunger; but abominable as they were and inhuman, He rather rehearses unto them a history.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Mark saith, “In the days of Abiathar the High Priest:”not stating what was con trary to the history, but implying that he had two names; and adds that “he gave unto him,”indicating that herein also David had much to say for himself, since even the very priest suffered him; and not only suffered, but even ministered unto him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me not that David was a prophet, for not even so was it lawful, but the privilege was the priests’: wherefore also He added, “but for the priests only.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though he were ten thousand times a prophet, yet was he not a priest; and though he were himself a prophet, yet not so they that were with him; since to them too we know that he gave.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then,” it might be said, “were they all one with David?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why talk to me of dignity, where there seems to be a transgression of the law, even though it be the constraint of nature?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and in this way too He hath the more entirely acquitted them of the charges, in that he who is greater is found to have done the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And what is this to the question,” one may say; “for it was not surely the Sabbath, that he transgressed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou tellest me of that which is greater, and which especially shows the wisdom of Christ, that letting go the Sabbath, He brings another example greater than the Sabbath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is by no means the same, to break in upon a day, and to touch that holy table, which it was not lawful for any man to touch.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since the Sabbath indeed hath been violated, and that often; nay rather it is continually being violated, both by circumcision, and by many other works; and at Jerichotoo one may see the same to have happened; but this happened then only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that He more than obtains the victory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then did no man blame David, although there was yet another ground of charge heavier than this, that of the priests’ murder, which had its origin from this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He states it not, as applying himself to the present subject only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Afterwards again He refutes it in another way also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as at first He brought in David, by the dignity of the person quelling their pride; so when He had stopped their mouths, and had put down their boasting, then He adds also the more appropriate refutation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And of what sort is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Know ye not, that in the temple the priests profane the Sabbath, and are blameless?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in that other instance indeed, saith He, the emergency made the relaxation, but here is the relaxation even without emergency.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He did not however at once thus refute them but first by way of permission, afterwards as insisting upon his argument.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because it was meet to draw the stronger inference last, although the former argument also had of course its proper weight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me not, that it is not freeing one’s self from blame, to bring forward another who is committing the same sin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when the doer incurs no blame, the act on which he hath ventured becomes a rule for others to plead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless He was not satisfied with this, but subjoins also what is more decisive, saying that the deed is no sin at all; and this more than anything was the sign of a glorious victory, to point to the law repealing itself, and in two ways doing so, first by the place, then by the Sabbath; or rather even in three ways, in that both the work is twofoldthat is done, and with it goes also another thing, its being done by the priests; and what is yet more, that it is not even brought as a charge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For they,” saith He, “are blameless.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how many points He hath stated? the place; for He saith, “In the temple;” the persons, for they are “the priests;” the time, for He saith, “the Sabbath;” the act itself, for “they profane;” (He not having said, “they break,” but what is more grievous, “they profane;”) that they not only escape punishment, but are even free from blame, “for they,” saith He, “are blameless.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not ye therefore account this, He saith, like the former instance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that indeed was done both but once, and not by a priest, and was of necessity; wherefore also they were deserving of excuse; but this last is both done every Sabbath, and by priests, and in the temple, and according to the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And therefore again not by favor, but in a legal way, they are acquitted of the charges.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not at all as blaming them did I so speak, saith He, nor yet as freeing them from blame in the way of indulgence, but according to the principle of justice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He seems indeed to be defending them, but it is His disciples whom He is clearing of the alleged faults.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when He saith, “those are blameless,” He means, “much more are these.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But they are not priests.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, they are greater than priests.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the Lord of the temple Himself is here: the truth, not the type.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He said also,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But I say unto you, That in this place is one greater than the temple.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, great as the sayings were which they heard, they made no reply, for the salvation of men was not their object.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, because to the hearers it would seem harsh, He quickly draws a veil over it, giving His discourse, as before, a lenient turn, yet even so expressing Himself with a rebuke.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But if ye had known what this meaneth, I will havemercy and not sacrifice, ye would not have condemned the guiltless.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how again He inclines His speech to lenity, yet again shows them to be out of the reach of lenity?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For ye would not have condemned,” saith He, “the guiltless.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Before indeed He inferred the same from what is said of the priests, in the words, “they are guiltless;” but here He states it on His own authority; or rather, this too is out of the law, for He was quoting a prophetic saying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this He mentions another reason likewise; “For the Son of man,” saith He, “is Lord of the Sabbath day;”speaking it of Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Mark relates Him to have said this of our common nature also; for He said, “The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then was he punished that was gathering the sticks?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because if the laws were to be despised even at the beginning, of course they would scarcely be observed afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed the Sabbath did at the first confer many and great benefits; for instance, it made them gentle towards those of their household, and humane; it taught them God’s providence and the creation, as Ezekiel saith;it trained them by degrees to abstain from wickedness, and disposed them to regard the things of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For because they could not have borne it,if when He was giving the law for the Sabbath, He had said, “Do your good works on the Sabbath, but do not the works which are evil,” therefore He restrained them from all alike for, “Ye must do nothing at all,” saith He: and not even so were they kept in order.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He Himself, in the very act of giving the law of the Sabbath, did even therein darkly signify that He will have them refrain from the evil works only, by the saying, “Ye must do no work, except what shall be done for your life.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in the temple too all went on, and with more diligence and double toil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus even by the very shadow He was secretly opening unto them the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did Christ then, it will be said, repeal a thing so highly profitable?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Far from it; nay, He greatly enhanced it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it was time for them to be trained in all things by the higher rules, and it was unnecessary that his hands should be bound, who was freed from wickedness, winged for all good works; or that men should hereby learn that God made all things; or that they should so be made gentle, who are called to imitate God’s own love to mankind (for He saith, “Be ye merciful, as your Heavenly Father”);or that they should make one day a festival, who are commanded to keep a feast all their life long; (“For let us keep the feast,” it is said, “not with old leaven, neither with leaven of malice and wickedness; but with unleavened bread of sincerity and truth”);as neither need they stand by an ark and a golden altar, who have the very Lord of all for their inmate, and in all things hold communion with Him; by prayer, and by oblation, and by scriptures, and by almsgiving, and by having Him within them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Lo now, why is any Sabbath required, by him who is always keeping the feast, whose conversation is in Heaven?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us keep the feast then continually, and do no evil thing; for this is a feast: and let our spiritual things be made intense, while our earthly things give place: and let us rest a spiritual rest, refraining our hands from covetousness; withdrawing our body from our superfluous and unprofitable toils, from such as the people of the Hebrews did of old endure in Egypt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is no difference betwixt us who are gathering gold, and those that were bound in the mire, working at those bricks, and gathering stubble, and being beaten.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for now too the devil bids us make bricks, as Pharaoh did then.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what else is gold, than mire? and what else is silver, than stubble?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Like stubble, at least, it kindles the flame of desire; like mire, so doth gold defile him that possesses it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He sent us, not Moses from the wilderness, but His Son from Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then, after He is come, thou abide in Egypt, thou wilt suffer with the Egyptians: but if leaving that land thou go up with the spiritual Israel, thou shalt see all the miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet not even this suffices for salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we must not only be delivered out of Egypt, but we must also enter into the promise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since the Jews too, as Paul saith, both went through the Red Sea,and ate manna, and drank spiritual drink, but nevertheless they all perished.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Lest then the same befall us also, let us not be slow, neither draw back; but when thou hearest wicked spies even now bringing up an evil report against the strait and narrow way, and uttering the same kind of talk as those spies of old, let not the multitude, but Joshua, be our pattern, and Caleb the son of Jephunneh; and do not thou give up, until thou have attained the promise, and entered into the Heavens.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Neither account the journey to be difficult.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For if when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God, much more, being reconciled, shall we be saved.”“But this way,” it will be said, “is strait and narrow.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well, but the former, through which thou hast come, is not strait and narrow only, but even impassable, and full of savage wild beasts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as there was no passing through the Red Sea, unless that miracle had been wrought, so neither could we, abiding in our former life, have gone up into Heaven, but only by baptism intervening.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if the impossible hath become possible, much more will the difficult be easy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But that,” it will be said, “was of grace only.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, for this reason especially thou hast just cause to take courage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, where it was grace alone, He wrought with you; will He not much more be your aid, where ye also show forth laborious works?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If He saved thee, doing nothing, will He not much more help thee, working?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Aboveindeed I was saying, that from the impossibilities thou oughtest to take courage about the difficulties also; but now I add this, that if we are vigilant, these will not be so much as difficult.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For mark it: death is trodden under foot, the devil hath fallen, the law of sin is extinguished, the grace of the Spirit is given, life is contracted into a small space, the heavy burdens are abridged.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to convince thee hereof by the actual results, see how many have overshot the injunctions of Christ; and art thou afraid of that which is just their measure?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What plea then wilt thou have, when others are leaping beyond the bounds, and thou thyself too slothful for what is enacted?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, thee we admonish to give alms of such things as thou hast, but another hath even stripped himself of all his possessions: thee we require to live chastely with thy wife, but another hath not so much as entered into marriage: and thee we entreat not to be envious, but another we find giving up even his own life for charity: thee again we entreat to be lenient in judgments, and not severe to them that sin, but another, even when smitten, hath turned the other cheek also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then shall we say, I pray thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What excuse shall we make, not doing even these things, when others go so far beyond us?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they would not have gone beyond us, had not the thing been very easy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For which pines away, he who envies other men’s blessings, or he who takes pleasure with them, and rejoices?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which eyes all things with suspicion and continual trembling, the chaste man, or the adulterer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which is cheered by good hopes, he that spoils by violence, or he that shows mercy, and imparts of his own to the needy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then bear in mind these things, and not be torpid in our career for virtue’s sake; but having stripped ourselves with all readiness for these glorious wrestlings, let us labor for a little while, that we may win the perpetual and imperishable crowns; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might forever and ever.
